title: Classifying shapes
description: Practice classifying shapes and determining which categories and subcategories each shape belongs to.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statement.** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Triangles are polygons with $3$ sides.

[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]] [[☃ image 3]] [[☃ image 4]]

Equilateral triangles are triangles with $3$ sides of equal length. 

[[☃ image 5]] [[☃ image 6]] [[☃ image 7]]

So, a triangle with $3$ sides of equal length is equilateral.  A triangle without $3$ sides of equal length is not equilateral.  
A triangle *could be* an equilateral triangle.

choices.content: A triangle is always an equilateral triangle.
A triangle could be an equilateral triangle.
A triangle is never an equilateral triangle.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statement.** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Polygons are simple, closed figures with $3$ or more sides.

[[☃ image 1]]  [[☃ image 2]] [[☃ image 3]]

Pentagons are simple closed figures with $5$ sides.  

[[☃ image 4]] [[☃ image 5]] [[☃ image 6]]

A pentagon *is always* a polygon.

choices.content: is always
could be
is never
A pentagon is always a polygon.
A pentagon could be a polygon.
A pentagon is never a polygon.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statement.** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Quadrilaterals are polygons with $4$ sides.

[[☃ image 1]]  [[☃ image 2]] [[☃ image 3]]

Rectangles are quadrilaterals with $4$ right angles.

[[☃ image 4]] [[☃ image 5]] [[☃ image 6]]
So, a quadrilateral with $4$ right angles is a rectangle.  A quadrilateral without $4$ right angles is not a rectangle.
A quadrilateral *could be* a rectangle.

choices.content: A quadrilateral is always a rectangle.
A quadrilateral could be a rectangle.
A quadrilateral is never a rectangle.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statement.** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Quadrilaterals are polygons with $4$ sides.

[[☃ image 7]] [[☃ image 8]] [[☃ image 9]]

Rhombuses are quadrilaterals with $4$ sides of equal length.

[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]] [[☃ image 3]]
A rhombus *is always* a quadrilateral.

choices.content: A rhombus is always a quadrilateral.
A rhombus could be a quadrilateral.
A rhombus is never a quadrilateral.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statement.** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Acute triangles have $3$ acute angles.

[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]] [[☃ image 3]]

Isosceles triangles have at least $2$ sides of equal length.  

[[☃ image 4]] [[☃ image 5]] [[☃ image 6]]
So, an acute triangle with $2$ equal sides is isosceles.  An acute triangle without $2$ sides of equal length is not isosceles.
An acute triangle *could be* an isosceles triangle.

choices.content: An acute triangle is always an isosceles triangle.
An acute triangle could be an isosceles triangle.
An acute triangle is never an isosceles triangle.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statement.** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Rectangles are quadrilaterals with $4$ right angles.

[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]] [[☃ image 3]]

Squares are quadrilaterals with $4$ right angles and $4$ sides of equal length.  

[[☃ image 4]] [[☃ image 5]] [[☃ image 6]]
So, a rectangle with $4$ sides of equal length is a square.  A rectangle without $4$ sides of equal length is not a square.
A rectangle *could be* a square.

choices.content: is always
could be
is never
A rectangle is always a square.
A rectangle could be a square.
A rectangle is never a square.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statement.** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Equilateral triangles have $3$ sides of equal length and $3$ acute angles.

[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]] [[☃ image 3]]

Acute triangles have $3$ acute angles.

[[☃ image 4]] [[☃ image 5]] [[☃ image 6]]
An equilateral triangle *is always*  an acute triangle.

choices.content: An equilateral triangle is always an acute triangle.
An equilateral triangle could be an acute triangle.
An equilateral triangle is never an acute triangle.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Select the true statement.** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Parallelograms are quadrilaterals, so they have $4$ sides.

[[☃ image 1]] [[☃ image 2]] [[☃ image 3]]

Hexagons are polygons with $6$ sides.    

[[☃ image 4]] [[☃ image 5]] [[☃ image 6]]
A parallelogram *is never* a hexagon.

choices.content: A parallelogram is always a hexagon.
A parallelogram could be a hexagon.
A parallelogram is never a hexagon.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements *must* be true about a parallelogram?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Let's look at a few parallelograms.  

[[☃ image 1]][[☃ image 2]][[☃ image 3]]

The matching arrow labels mean that two opposite sides are parallel.

A parellelogram *must* have $\maroonD{\text{two pairs of parallel opposite sides}}$.

A parallelogram can *sometimes* have $\blueE{\text{four right angles}}$. 

[[☃ image 1]]

However, we can draw a parallelogram without four right angles.

[[☃ image 2]]

So, *no* we cannot say that a parallelogram must have right angles.

A parallelogram can *sometimes* have $\purpleD{\text{four sides of equal length}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

However, we can draw a parallelogram without four sides of equal length.

[[☃ image 2]]

So, *no* we cannot say that a parallelogram must have four sides of equal length.
A parallelogram must have two pairs of parallel opposite sides.

choices.content: It has four sides of equal length.
It has four right angles.
It has two pairs of parallel opposite sides.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements *must* be true about a rhombus?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Let's look at a few rhombuses.  

[[☃ image 1]][[☃ image 2]][[☃ image 3]]

The matching arrow labels indicate that two opposite sides are parallel.

A rhombus **must** have $\goldE{\text{two pairs of parallel opposite sides}}$.
The rhombus **must** have $\purpleE{\text{four sides of equal length}}$.

A rhombus can have $\blueE{\text{four right angles}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

However, we can draw a rhombus without four right angles.

[[☃ image 2]]

So, *no* we cannot say that a rhombus must have four right angles.

A rhombus must have 

- Four sides of equal length 
- Two pairs of parallel opposite sides

choices.content: It has four sides of equal length.
It has four right angles.
It has two pairs of parallel opposite sides.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements *must* be true about a square?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's look at a few squares.  

[[☃ image 1]][[☃ image 2]][[☃ image 3]]

The matching arrow labels indicate that two opposite sides are parallel.

A square **must** have $\goldE{\text{two pairs of parallel opposite sides}}$.
The square **must** have $\blueE{\text{four right angles}}$.
The square **must** have $\purpleE{\text{four side of equal length.}}$

A square must have 

- Four right angles
- Four sides of equal length
- Two pairs of parallel opposite sides

choices.content: It has four sides of equal length.
It has four right angles.
It has two pairs of parallel opposite sides.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements *must* be true about a rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Let's look at a few rectangles.  

[[☃ image 1]][[☃ image 2]][[☃ image 3]]

The matching arrow labels indicate that two opposite sides are parallel.

A rectangle **must** have $\goldE{\text{two pairs of parallel opposite sides}}$.
The rectangle **must** have $\blueE{\text{four right angles}}$.

A rectangle can *sometimes* have $\purpleE{\text{four sides of equal length}}$. 

[[☃ image 1]]

However, we can draw a rectangle without four sides of equal length.

[[☃ image 2]]

So, *no* we cannot say that a rectangle must have four sides of equal length.

A rectangle must have 

- Four right angles   
- Two pairs of parallel opposite sides

choices.content: It has four sides of equal length.
It has four right angles.
It has two pairs of parallel opposite sides.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kim sorted shapes by their characteristics using the  [[☃ definition 1]]  below.

**What characteristic is the same about all of the shapes in each circle but different from all of the other shapes *not* in that circle? **

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
##Labeling the left circle

Each figure in the left circle is a **regular polygon** because all of the sides in the figure have the same length and all of the angles have the same measure. 

None of the figures outside of the circle have sides with all equal lengths. 

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

##Labeling the right circle

Each figure in the right circle is a **triangle** because it has $3$ sides. 

None of the figures outside of the circle have $3$ sides.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This is the labeled diagram:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.definition: A Venn diagram is a visual tool that groups items in a set by what they have in common.

options.imageAlt: Venn diagram with rectangular region that fully surrounds two partially overlapping circular regions. In the non-overlapping part of the left circle, there is a figure with 8 equal sides and a figure with 5 equal sides. In the overlap between the two circles, there is a figure with 3 equal sides. In the non-overlapping part of the right circle, there is a figure with 3 unequal sides and one right angle. In the rectangular region but outside of both circles, there is a figure with 4 unequal sides, with 2 sides parallel.

assessment_items.question_content:
Claire sorted shapes using the [[☃ definition 1]] below.  

**What characteristic is the same about all of the shapes in each circle but different from all of the other shapes *not* in that circle? **

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
##Labeling the left circle

Each figure in the left circle is a **rectangle** because the figures have four right angles and two pairs of parallel sides.

None of the figures outside of the circle have right angles.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

##Labeling the right circle

Each figure in the right circle is a **rhombus** because it has $4$ equal sides. 

None of the figures outside of the circle have $4$ equal sides.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 2]]

This is the labeled diagram:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.definition: A Venn diagram is a visual tool that groups items in a set by what they have in common.

options.imageAlt: Venn diagram with rectangular region that fully surrounds two partially overlapping circular regions. In the non-overlapping part of the left circle, there is a figure with 2 pairs of parallel sides and 4 right angles. In the overlap between the two circles, there is a figure with 4 equal sides and 4 right angles. In the non-overlapping part of the right circle, there is a figure with 4 equal sides and no right angles. In the rectangular region but outside of both circles, there is a figure with 2 pairs of parallel sides and no right angles.

assessment_items.question_content:
Beckett sorted shapes using the [[☃ definition 1]] below.  

**What characteristic is the same about all of the shapes in each circle but different from all of the other shapes *not* in that circle? **

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
##Labeling the ellipse

Each figure in the ellipse is a **quadrilateral** because the figures have four straight sides and are closed figures.

None of the figures outside of the ellipse are closed figures.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

##Labeling the circle

Each figure in the circle is a **parallelogram** because it has $2$ pairs of parallel sides. 

None of the figures outside of the circle have $2$ pairs of parallel sides.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

This is the labeled diagram:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.definition: A Venn diagram is a visual tool that groups items in a set by what they have in common.

options.imageAlt: Venn diagram with rectangular region that fully surrounds an ellipse-shaped region and a circular region. The circle is fully inside the ellipse. In the non-overlapping part of the ellipse, there is a closed figure with 4 straight sides; and a figure with 4 sides, one pair of parallel sides, and 2 right angles. In the circle, there is a figure with 2 pairs of parallel sides and 4 right angles; and a figure with 2 pairs of parallel sides. In the rectangular region but outside of the ellipse, there is an open figure with 4 straight sides.

assessment_items.question_content:
Blaise sorted shapes using the [[☃ definition 1]] below.  

**What characteristic is the same about all of the shapes in each circle but different from all of the other shapes *not* in that circle? **

[[☃ label-image 1]]
hints:
##Labeling the left circle

Each figure in the left circle is a **right triangle** because the figures all have $3$ sides and a right angle. 

None of the figures outside of the circle have right angles. 

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

##Labeling the right circle

Each figure in the right circle is an **isosceles triangle** because it has $3$ sides, with at least $2$ equal sides. 

None of the figures outside of the circle have $2$ equal sides.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

This is the labeled diagram:

[[☃ image 1]]

options.definition: A Venn diagram is a visual tool that groups items in a set by what they have in common.

options.imageAlt: Venn diagram with rectangular region that fully surrounds two partially overlapping circular regions. In the non-overlapping part of the left circle, there is a figure with 3 sides and a right angle. In the overlap between the two circles, there is a figure with 3 total sides, 2 of which are equal, and a right angle. In the non-overlapping part of the right circle, there is a figure with 3 equal sides; and a figure with 3 total sides, 2 of which are equal. In the rectangular region but outside of both circles, there is a figure with 3 unequal sides.

assessment_items.question_content:
Parallelogram has $2$ pairs of opposite sides that are parallel.

The opposite sides of a rhombus are parallel.

**Is a rhombus also a parallelogram?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A $\blueE{\text{parallelogram}}$ is a quadrilateral with $\blueE{\text{2 pairs of parallel sides}}$.
A  $\maroonE{\text{rhombus}}$ has $\blueE{\text{2 pairs of parallel sides}}$ and $\maroonE{\text{4 sides of equal length}}$.
Yes, a rhombus is a parallelogram.

choices.content: Yes
No
Not enough information

assessment_items.question_content:
A rectangle has $2$ pairs of opposite sides that are equal.

All sides in a square are equal.

**Is a square also a rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A $\blueE{\text{rectangle}}$ is a quadrilateral with $\blueE{\text{2 pairs of opposite sides that are equal}}$.
A  $\maroonE{\text{square}}$ has $\maroonE{\text{4 sides of equal length}}$. If all $4$ are equal, then opposite ones must be equal, too.
Yes, a square is a rectangle.

choices.content: Yes
No
Not enough information

assessment_items.question_content:
A rhombus has $2$ pairs of parallel sides and $4$ sides of equal length.

A rectangle has $2$ pairs of parallel sides and $2$ pairs of opposite sides that are equal.

**Is a rectangle also a rhombus?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A  $\maroonE{\text{rhombus}}$ has $\blueE{\text{2 pairs of parallel sides}}$ and $\maroonE{\text{4 sides of equal length}}$.
A $\goldE{\text{rectangle}}$ is a quadrilateral with $\goldE{\text{2 pairs of opposite sides that are equal}}$.
No, a rectangle is not a rhombus.

choices.content: Yes
No
Not enough information

assessment_items.question_content:
A rhombus has $2$ pairs of opposite sides that are parallel and $4$ equal sides.

A trapezoid has $4$ sides and $4$ angles.

**Is a trapezoid also a rhombus?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A  $\maroonE{\text{rhombus}}$ has $\blueE{\text{2 pairs of parallel sides}}$ and $\maroonE{\text{4 sides of equal length}}$.
A  $\goldE{\text{trapezoid}}$ has $\goldE{\text{4 sides}}$ and $\goldE{\text{4 angles}}$.
We do not have enough information to determine if a trapezoid is a rhombus.

choices.content: Yes
No
Not enough information
